course_info.title: Statistical Methods in Psychology
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

learning_objectives: Calculate and interpret measures of central tendency and variability
Create appropriate graphs for different types of data
Identify skewness, kurtosis, and outliers in distributions
readings: Gravetter & Wallnau, Statistics for the Behavioral Sciences, Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Statistics is the language of research. This course gives you the quantitative tools to analyze behavioral data and draw meaningful conclusions.
key_concepts: Mean, median, mode: when to use each
Range, variance, standard deviation
Normal distribution and the empirical rule (68-95-99.7)
z-scores and standard normal distribution
Histograms, box plots, and scatter plots
lecture_notes.demonstration: Using SPSS to compute descriptive statistics and generate visualizations from a sample dataset of exam scores

review.value: The average distance of scores from the mean; larger SD indicates more variability in the data
z = (X - M) / SD; transforms raw scores to a standard scale for comparison across distributions

learning_objectives: Apply basic probability rules to psychological research scenarios
Explain the central limit theorem and its importance
Describe the sampling distribution of the mean

lecture_notes.introduction: Inferential statistics rests on probability theory and the behavior of sampling distributions. Understanding these foundations is critical for hypothesis testing.
key_concepts: Probability: frequentist interpretation
Sampling with and without replacement
Central limit theorem: sample means approach normality as n increases
Standard error of the mean: SE = SD / sqrt(n)
Law of large numbers
lecture_notes.demonstration: Simulation: draw repeated samples from a skewed population and observe the sampling distribution becoming normal
setup_requirements: Sampling simulation applet or R script

review.value: Regardless of population shape, the sampling distribution of the mean approaches normal with sufficient sample size (n >= 30)
The standard deviation of the sampling distribution; decreases as sample size increases

class_schedule.topic: Hypothesis Testing: One-Sample and Independent-Samples t-Tests
learning_objectives: State null and alternative hypotheses for a given research question
Conduct and interpret one-sample and independent-samples t-tests
Distinguish between Type I and Type II errors

lecture_notes.introduction: Hypothesis testing is the formal procedure for deciding whether sample data provide sufficient evidence to reject a null hypothesis about a population.
key_concepts: Null hypothesis (H0) and alternative hypothesis (H1)
Alpha level, critical values, and rejection regions
One-sample t-test: comparing a sample mean to a known population value
Independent-samples t-test: comparing two group means
Type I error (alpha) and Type II error (beta); statistical power
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compute an independent-samples t-test by hand and in SPSS: comparing exam scores for two study methods

review.value: The probability of obtaining the observed result (or more extreme) if the null hypothesis is true; reject H0 when p < alpha
d = (M1 - M2) / pooled SD; small = 0.2, medium = 0.5, large = 0.8

class_schedule.topic: ANOVA: Comparing Three or More Groups
learning_objectives: Explain when ANOVA is preferred over multiple t-tests
Conduct and interpret a one-way between-subjects ANOVA
Perform post-hoc comparisons using Tukey's HSD

lecture_notes.introduction: When comparing three or more groups, ANOVA controls the family-wise error rate that would inflate with multiple t-tests.
key_concepts: Between-groups variance vs. within-groups variance
F-ratio: MS_between / MS_within
Degrees of freedom for ANOVA
Post-hoc tests: Tukey HSD, Bonferroni correction
Effect size: eta-squared and partial eta-squared
lecture_notes.demonstration: One-way ANOVA in SPSS: does therapy type (CBT, medication, combined) affect depression scores?

review.value: Ratio of systematic variance (between groups) to error variance (within groups); larger F suggests group differences
Post-hoc test that compares all possible pairs of means while controlling family-wise alpha

learning_objectives: Calculate and interpret Pearson's r correlation coefficient
Conduct a simple linear regression and interpret the output
Understand the distinction between correlation and prediction

lecture_notes.introduction: Correlation quantifies the strength and direction of linear relationships. Regression extends this to prediction, allowing us to estimate one variable from another.
key_concepts: Pearson r: formula, range (-1 to +1), interpretation
Coefficient of determination (r-squared)
Simple linear regression: Y' = bX + a
Least squares criterion
Residuals and assumptions of regression
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compute Pearson r and regression equation for study hours predicting exam scores; interpret in SPSS

review.value: The proportion of variance in Y explained by X; e.g., r = 0.60 means r-squared = 0.36, so 36% of variance is shared
Y' = bX + a, where b is the slope (change in Y per unit change in X) and a is the y-intercept

learning_objectives: Conduct and interpret chi-square goodness-of-fit and independence tests
Identify when non-parametric tests are appropriate
Apply the Mann-Whitney U and Wilcoxon signed-rank tests

lecture_notes.introduction: Not all data are continuous and normally distributed. Chi-square and non-parametric tests handle categorical data and violations of parametric assumptions.
key_concepts: Chi-square goodness-of-fit: observed vs. expected frequencies
Chi-square test of independence: two categorical variables
Cramers V for effect size with chi-square
Mann-Whitney U: non-parametric alternative to independent t-test
Wilcoxon signed-rank: non-parametric alternative to paired t-test
lecture_notes.demonstration: Chi-square test of independence: is therapy completion (yes/no) related to therapy type? Compute by hand and in SPSS

review.value: Compares observed frequencies to expected frequencies under the null hypothesis; df = (rows-1)(cols-1) for independence test
Use when data violate normality, are ordinal, or have small samples; trade some power for fewer assumptions

questions.question: A sample of 25 therapy clients has a mean anxiety score of 42 with a standard deviation of 8. The population mean for untreated individuals is 48. Conduct a one-sample t-test at alpha = .05 and state your conclusion.
questions.answer: t = (42 - 48) / (8 / sqrt(25)) = -6 / 1.6 = -3.75. With df = 24 and alpha = .05 (two-tailed), the critical t is approximately +/- 2.064. Since |-3.75| > 2.064, we reject the null hypothesis. The therapy group has significantly lower anxiety scores than the untreated population.

rubric.key: Correct degrees of freedom and critical value

questions.question: A researcher reports a study with p = .03 and concludes there is a 97% chance the alternative hypothesis is true. Is this interpretation correct? Explain.
questions.answer: No, this is incorrect. The p-value is the probability of obtaining the observed data (or more extreme) assuming the null hypothesis is true. It is NOT the probability that the null hypothesis is false or that the alternative is true. The correct interpretation: if the null were true, there is a 3% chance of seeing results this extreme. The p-value says nothing about the probability of the hypotheses themselves.

rubric.key: Explains the distinction between data probability and hypothesis probability

questions.question: An ANOVA yields F(2, 27) = 5.12, p = .013. There are three groups of 10 participants each. Calculate eta-squared and interpret the result.
questions.answer: Eta-squared = SS_between / SS_total. From F = MS_between / MS_within and df_between = 2, df_within = 27: eta-squared can be approximated as (F * df_between) / (F * df_between + df_within) = (5.12 * 2) / (5.12 * 2 + 27) = 10.24 / 37.24 = 0.275. About 27.5% of the variance in the dependent variable is accounted for by group membership, which is a large effect by Cohen's guidelines.

questions.question: A researcher reports r = .45 between hours of exercise per week and self-reported happiness. What is r-squared, what does it mean, and what caution should be stated about interpreting this finding?
questions.answer: r-squared = .45^2 = .2025, meaning approximately 20% of the variance in happiness is shared with (or explained by) exercise. The caution: correlation does not establish causation. It is possible that happier people exercise more (reverse causality), or that a third variable (e.g., overall health, socioeconomic status) drives both exercise and happiness.

rubric.key: States correlation does not equal causation with example

major_assignments.title: Statistical Analysis Report
major_assignments.description: Using a provided dataset of 200 participants in a therapy outcome study, conduct a complete statistical analysis including descriptive statistics, t-tests, ANOVA, correlation, and regression. Present findings in APA format with tables and figures.
objectives: Demonstrate proficiency with multiple statistical techniques
Select appropriate analyses for different research questions
Interpret results in the context of psychological research
Present statistical findings clearly in APA format
deliverables: SPSS output files for all analyses

criteria: Correct computation of all statistical tests
Appropriate test selection for each research question
Proper handling of assumptions and violations
Accurate reporting of test statistics, df, p-values, effect sizes
Correct interpretation of each statistical result
Clear narrative connecting results to hypotheses
Accurate computation and appropriate selection of statistical tests
Insightful interpretation connecting results to psychological research questions
Professional APA-formatted tables, figures, and narrative
Demonstrates statistical literacy and critical thinking

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of statistical concepts and computation skills

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on SPSS exercises applying statistical techniques to real datasets

major_assignments.description: Comprehensive statistical analysis report in APA format

midterm_examination.description: Exam covering descriptive statistics through ANOVA

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all statistical methods

course_policies.attendance: Lab attendance is mandatory. Students who miss more than 2 labs without documentation will lose 5% from their final grade per additional absence.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late lab reports are penalized 10% per day. The major assignment will not be accepted more than 3 days late.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab partners may work together on exercises, but each student must submit their own written analysis. Copying SPSS output interpretations is considered academic dishonesty.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Fabrication of data or statistical results is a serious violation. All analyses must be conducted on the provided datasets.
course_policies.technology_requirements: SPSS or JASP statistical software (available in campus labs and via student license), scientific calculator, and access to statistical tables.
